claim: Dogs have 220–300 million olfactory receptors versus 5–6 million in humans, and an olfactory bulb 40 times larger, enabling scent detection at concentrations and trail ages far beyond human capability.
stance_target: Justification for using scent-based search strategies for lost dogs
quoted_support: Their noses contain between 220 to 300 million olfactory receptors, whereas humans only have about 5 to 6 million... the olfactory bulb in dogs' brains, which processes smell, is 40 times larger than that of humans.

claim: Human scent is composed of skin flakes, sweat, sebum, and bacterial metabolites — each person has a unique fingerprint-like odor profile detectable even from tiny residues over considerable distances and time periods.
stance_target: Mechanism by which SAR dogs track specific individuals in lost dog rescue
scope_conditions: General; applies to any human or animal with distinct skin microbiome
quoted_support: Human scent comprises a unique mixture of elements such as skin flakes, sweat, sebum, and bacterial byproducts. Each person has a distinct odor profile, almost like a fingerprint, which allows trained dogs to identify and follow specific individuals even in crowded or challenging environments.

claim: Warm and humid conditions are optimal for scent preservation, while cold and dry conditions cause scent particles to disperse faster; hot and dry conditions also accelerate scent dissipation.
stance_target: Algarve summer scent search conditions — high heat and low humidity reduce effective scent range
scope_conditions: SAR operations; cites Rivera & Meyers-Manor 2024 and Pickersgill et al. 2023
quoted_support: In warmer and more humid conditions, scent particles tend to remain intact for longer periods. The moisture in the air helps to keep the scent particles from dissipating quickly... in extremely dry conditions, scent particles can evaporate faster, reducing the ability for dogs to detect them effectively.

claim: Grass and soft soil retain scent significantly better than concrete, asphalt, rocky or sandy surfaces, and urban cross-contamination further degrades tracking ability in built environments.
stance_target: Terrain-differentiated search strategy for Algarve mixed terrain (scrub, dirt, village roads)
quoted_support: Forests and Grasslands: These environments generally hold onto scent particles well because the vegetation can trap the particles... Rocky and Sandy Surfaces: These types of terrain can complicate scent retention as the particles can get buried or dispersed quickly.

claim: Air-scenting (nose high, detects airborne particles) is most effective in open areas and requires no fixed start point, while ground tracking (nose low, follows footstep odors) requires a known last location and works best on grass and soft soil.
stance_target: SAR method selection based on terrain type and last known position availability
quoted_support: Air-scenting is most effective when there's a need to search for individuals in vast, open spaces. The key advantage is that the dog doesn't require a distinct starting point as it can pick up scents carried through the air.

claim: Variable wind makes the scent trail unpredictable and disperses scent concentration over a broader area, reducing detection reliability; calm wind produces a more concentrated and followable trail.
stance_target: Effect of Nortada gusts on search window timing in Algarve
quoted_support: Variable Wind: Makes the scent disperse over a broader area, creating a more challenging situation for the dog, as the concentration of scent particles reduces with the distance they are carried.

claim: SAR training begins at 8 weeks old with scent recognition exercises; dogs must learn to differentiate fresh vs. aged scent and ignore competing animal, food, and human odors.
stance_target: SAR dog capability baseline for lost dog rescue operations
quoted_support: From around 8 weeks old, SAR puppies engage in scent recognition exercises... They practice ignoring distractions such as animal scents, food smells, and other human odors.
